description: Seed transcripts for live-suggestion eval. Each fixture is a 60-90s meeting slice with plantable signals the suggestions pipeline is expected to surface. Use alongside docs/evals/rubric.md to grade output.

signal_legend.claims: Verifiable statements (number, date, capability, commitment made as fact). Rubric expects fact_check candidates.
signal_legend.questions_asked: Explicit questions in-transcript that went unanswered. Rubric expects answer-type suggestions.
signal_legend.commitments: Dates, owners, deliverables named as decided. Rubric expects probes that test hidden assumptions.
signal_legend.ambiguous: Phrases with multiple meanings or undefined scope. Rubric expects clarification-type suggestions.
signal_legend.objections: Signals of doubt, resistance, or competing priorities. Rubric expects objection-handling talking_points.
signal_legend.unresolved_threads: Named topics raised and dropped without closure. Rubric expects forward-looking questions.

fixtures.description: Mid-funnel SaaS discovery. Prospect is researching but evasive on budget and authority.

questions_asked: What does the rollout timeline look like on your side?
unresolved_threads: who signs off on the purchase
actual budget ceiling

transcript.speaker: AE
transcript.text: Appreciate you taking the call. Before we dive into the platform, it would help to understand what prompted you to look at alternatives now.

transcript.speaker: Prospect
transcript.text: Yeah, we're exploring options. We have a team of 40 engineers and the current tool is getting expensive. We pay about 90k a year for it and the ROI isn't obvious anymore.

transcript.speaker: AE
transcript.text: Got it. When you say the ROI isn't obvious, is that a utilization question or an outcome question?

transcript.speaker: Prospect
transcript.text: Bit of both. Half the team doesn't log in regularly. And we haven't tied it to any shipping metric.

transcript.speaker: AE
transcript.text: That tracks with what we see. What does the rollout timeline look like on your side if you did decide to switch?

transcript.speaker: Prospect
transcript.text: Sometime this quarter probably. I'd need to align with Priya on engineering leadership and obviously finance would have to look at the numbers.

transcript.speaker: AE
transcript.text: Makes sense. Is Priya the person who would ultimately say yes or no?

transcript.speaker: Prospect
transcript.text: She has a lot of say. I pull the trigger on things under 100k but anything above she and Marcus in finance have to bless it together.

fixtures.description: Late-stage negotiation. Champion raises a security objection and a pricing objection in the same turn.

claims: contract would be 140k for year one
questions_asked: Can you do 120 flat for year one if we commit to two years?
objections: security team nervous about data residency
unresolved_threads: what specifically in data residency is blocking
two-year commitment vs discount

transcript.speaker: Champion
transcript.text: Okay, look, I want to make this work but I have two problems. First, my security team is nervous about data residency and second, the pricing is higher than I budgeted for.

transcript.speaker: AE
transcript.text: Let's take them one at a time. On security, what specifically has them concerned? We're SOC 2 Type 2 and the data stays in the region you pick.

transcript.speaker: Champion
transcript.text: I know you're SOC 2, their tool is also SOC 2 Type 2. The issue they raised was about where the vector embeddings get processed. Apparently some vendors ship them back to a different region for training.

transcript.speaker: AE
transcript.text: Understood. We don't train on customer data at all, and embeddings stay in your region. I can send the architecture doc that your team can review.

transcript.speaker: Champion
transcript.text: That would help. On the pricing, the contract you sent is 140k for year one. Can you do 120 flat for year one if we commit to two years?

transcript.speaker: AE
transcript.text: I can take that to my VP. If I get you to 120, what does the signing timeline look like?

transcript.speaker: Champion
transcript.text: If the architecture doc checks out with security, I can get procurement moving next week.

fixtures.description: Senior engineer interview. Candidate gives a vague accomplishment that hides scope and personal contribution.

unresolved_threads: what specifically broke before the migration
how the candidate personally contributed vs delegated

transcript.speaker: Interviewer
transcript.text: Tell me about a technical project you're proud of in the last two years.

transcript.speaker: Candidate
transcript.text: Sure. The biggest one was the checkout platform migration. It was slow and had reliability issues. I led the migration to a new service architecture and we cut latency by 60 percent.

transcript.speaker: Interviewer
transcript.text: When you say you led it, what did that mean in practice?

transcript.speaker: Candidate
transcript.text: I wrote the design doc, got buy-in from the platform team, and made sure we shipped on time. A team of four engineers worked under me.

transcript.speaker: Interviewer
transcript.text: What was actually breaking before the migration?

transcript.speaker: Candidate
transcript.text: A lot of things. Database contention, slow external calls, that kind of thing. We made it faster and more reliable.

transcript.speaker: Interviewer
transcript.text: Which of those did you personally dig into first?

transcript.speaker: Candidate
transcript.text: I stabilized the platform mostly by moving payments off the monolith. That was the biggest win. Then we handled the other bottlenecks.

fixtures.description: Candidate defends a tradeoff decision under pressure. Strong signal for probe-into-rejected-alternative.

claims: Postgres would not scale to our write volume
questions_asked: Did you benchmark Postgres with partitioning before ruling it out?
objections: interviewer pushes back on the conclusion
unresolved_threads: what numbers defined 'would not scale'
what got rejected

transcript.speaker: Interviewer
transcript.text: Talk me through the datastore decision for the event pipeline.

transcript.speaker: Candidate
transcript.text: We looked at Postgres first because we already ran it. But Postgres would not scale to our write volume. So we picked Cassandra.

transcript.speaker: Interviewer
transcript.text: When you say it wouldn't scale, what number did you have in mind?

transcript.speaker: Candidate
transcript.text: We were projecting around 80 thousand writes per second at peak. That's a lot for a single Postgres instance.

transcript.speaker: Interviewer
transcript.text: Did you benchmark Postgres with partitioning before ruling it out?

transcript.speaker: Candidate
transcript.text: We did a light test with pg_partman. It got to maybe 40k before latency ballooned. We didn't do a full capacity test with tuned hardware, admittedly.

transcript.speaker: Interviewer
transcript.text: So what did Cassandra buy you that was decisive?

transcript.speaker: Candidate
transcript.text: Horizontal scaling without the partitioning operational overhead. But the tradeoff was losing joins and having weaker consistency guarantees.

fixtures.description: Architecture review. Proposal claims a specific throughput that should be stress-tested before accepting.

claims: Kafka handles 200k messages per second on three brokers
questions_asked: What happens when a broker fails mid-publish?
ambiguous: we can always add more brokers
unresolved_threads: replication factor and consumer lag under failure
p99 claim under packet loss

transcript.speaker: Presenter
transcript.text: For the notifications pipeline we're proposing Kafka with three brokers. Kafka handles 200k messages per second on three brokers based on the load tests we ran last sprint.

transcript.speaker: Reviewer
transcript.text: What was the message size in those load tests?

transcript.speaker: Presenter
transcript.text: About 1 kilobyte. Similar to the real payload shape.

transcript.speaker: Reviewer
transcript.text: What happens when a broker fails mid-publish? Did you measure consumer lag during that scenario?

transcript.speaker: Presenter
transcript.text: We didn't do an explicit failure test. But with replication factor 3 it should be fine. We can always add more brokers if needed.

transcript.speaker: Reviewer
transcript.text: And then the fanout — you said the end-to-end adds 15ms p99 to websocket clients.

transcript.speaker: Presenter
transcript.text: Yes, 15ms p99 fanout. That's with the current connection count of about 80 thousand open sockets.

transcript.speaker: Reviewer
transcript.text: Measured in what network conditions? And was that sustained or a burst average?

fixtures.description: Incident postmortem. Team is converging on a root cause that may be incomplete.

claims: the outage was caused by the config push
commitments: we'll add a config schema validator
ambiguous: something was off with the rollback
unresolved_threads: why the canary didn't catch it
whether the fix addresses the class of issue

transcript.speaker: Lead
transcript.text: Okay so to summarize, the outage was caused by the config push at 14:02. The rollback completed in four minutes and traffic recovered by 14:08.

transcript.speaker: SRE
transcript.text: Yeah. We'll add a config schema validator in the deploy pipeline. That would have caught this one.

transcript.speaker: Engineer
transcript.text: Something was off with the rollback though. It took longer than our drill numbers suggested. I think it was four minutes to recovery but only because we manually intervened.

transcript.speaker: Lead
transcript.text: Good flag. Can you add that to the follow-ups? Let's make sure the automated rollback path actually works under the same load pattern.

transcript.speaker: SRE
transcript.text: And the canary didn't catch it either. It was a 5 percent canary but the failure mode only showed up at full fanout.

transcript.speaker: Lead
transcript.text: Right. I want the action items to be the schema validator, the canary fanout review, and a rollback drill. Who owns which?

transcript.speaker: SRE
transcript.text: I'll take the schema validator. Drill ownership should probably sit with the on-call rotation.

fixtures.description: Daily standup. One engineer buries a blocker inside a status update.

claims: I should be done by Thursday
unresolved_threads: what specifically is blocking QA
who owns the backend fix

transcript.speaker: PM
transcript.text: Quick round. Jamie, what's your status?

transcript.speaker: Jamie
transcript.text: I'm on the search filters ticket. Making progress, working through some stuff with the highlight logic. I should be done by Thursday.

transcript.speaker: PM
transcript.text: Cool. Priya?

transcript.speaker: Priya
transcript.text: Finished the instrumentation work yesterday. Today I'm picking up the vendor integration. No blockers.

transcript.speaker: PM
transcript.text: Great. Sam?

transcript.speaker: Sam
transcript.text: QA side of the payments redesign. We're kind of stuck, honestly. Waiting on backend to return the right error codes. Been waiting since Monday.

transcript.speaker: PM
transcript.text: Which endpoint specifically?

transcript.speaker: Sam
transcript.text: The refund flow. When the backend returns a 500 we can't tell if it's a retryable timeout or a real failure. We asked backend on Monday but nothing yet.

transcript.speaker: PM
transcript.text: Okay. Anything else to flag?

fixtures.description: Product brainstorm. Team is generating ideas with fuzzy user definitions.

claims: new managers don't know who to ask
questions_asked: What if the onboarding assistant surfaced it automatically?
unresolved_threads: who the actual buyer is vs the user
what 'stuff they care about' means

transcript.speaker: Designer
transcript.text: Okay, the brief is onboarding for first-time managers. What are we actually solving?

transcript.speaker: PM
transcript.text: New managers don't know who to ask when they hit something unfamiliar. They're guessing. We want to reduce that.

transcript.speaker: Designer
transcript.text: What if the onboarding assistant surfaced it automatically when someone opened a screen they hadn't seen before?

transcript.speaker: PM
transcript.text: I like that direction. And we could personalize it based on what matters to them, like stuff they care about in their week one.

transcript.speaker: Engineer
transcript.text: Are we building this for new managers or for power users? Because a power user would hate a prompt every time they open a new screen.

transcript.speaker: PM
transcript.text: Just new managers for now. Week one and two specifically.

transcript.speaker: Designer
transcript.text: Who's the buyer though? If HR is pushing this, the design will be different than if the manager themselves requests it.

fixtures.description: Release planning. Team is committing to a June 15 launch with a hidden upstream dependency.

unresolved_threads: what happens if vendor API slips
what slips first if design misses freeze

transcript.speaker: TPM
transcript.text: Okay let's lock the June 15 launch. Design freeze is May 1, engineering feature-complete May 22, QA needs two weeks so we're cut by June 5.

transcript.speaker: Design Lead
transcript.text: May 1 is tight for the dashboard revamp. Can we carve out the reports page as phase 2?

transcript.speaker: TPM
transcript.text: If we carve reports out, does phase 1 still land on June 15?

transcript.speaker: Design Lead
transcript.text: Yes, without reports we can hit May 1.

transcript.speaker: Eng Lead
transcript.text: I have a couple of risks to flag. The vendor API we depend on for the auth piece hasn't confirmed their May 20 readiness date. If they slip, we slip.

transcript.speaker: TPM
transcript.text: Did we get that in writing?

transcript.speaker: Eng Lead
transcript.text: Verbal only. Their account rep said early May but didn't commit to a specific date.

transcript.speaker: TPM
transcript.text: Okay. We're committing to June 15 for phase 1. I want the vendor date confirmed in writing by Friday.

fixtures.description: Manager-report 1:1. Report is indirectly raising a scope-and-burnout concern.

unresolved_threads: which part of the new scope is unsustainable
whether the career path conversation is still pending

transcript.speaker: Manager
transcript.text: How's the new scope going? Last time we talked it was pretty fresh.

transcript.speaker: Report
transcript.text: It's going. I mean, it's a lot. But I'm managing.

transcript.speaker: Manager
transcript.text: What does 'a lot' look like day to day?

transcript.speaker: Report
transcript.text: The infra work I was already doing, plus now the on-call rotation, plus the mentorship for the two new hires. The new stuff is different from what I was good at.

transcript.speaker: Manager
transcript.text: Which piece feels hardest?

transcript.speaker: Report
transcript.text: Honestly the mentorship. I don't feel like I'm doing it well. The infra stuff I can execute in my sleep.

transcript.speaker: Manager
transcript.text: What would good mentorship look like if you defined it?

transcript.speaker: Report
transcript.text: I don't know. I haven't had time to figure that out. Also, we were supposed to circle back on the staff eng track thing. That kind of fell off.

fixtures.description: Meeting just started. Context is thin but a specific promise has already been made.

claims: we shipped the new onboarding last Thursday
commitments: Marco will prepare the dashboard by next Friday
unresolved_threads: what 'interesting' means
whether dashboard scope is defined

transcript.speaker: Host
transcript.text: Thanks everyone for joining. The purpose of this meeting is to align on the next two weeks.

transcript.speaker: Host
transcript.text: Quick update from me: we shipped the new onboarding last Thursday. The numbers are looking interesting, not definitive yet.

transcript.speaker: Marco
transcript.text: I can put together a proper dashboard on the onboarding funnel. I'll have it by next Friday.

transcript.speaker: Host
transcript.text: Perfect. We'll use it to decide whether to roll forward or adjust.

transcript.speaker: Amira
transcript.text: Should we get any input from support on early signals? They were flagging some confusion last week.

transcript.speaker: Host
transcript.text: Yes. Let's add that to Marco's dashboard if we can pull tagged support tickets.

transcript.speaker: Marco
transcript.text: Sure. I'll sync with support Thursday.

fixtures.description: Unstructured discussion with mixed signals. Designed to be genuinely ambiguous to classify.

claims: the support tickets dropped 30% after the redesign
questions_asked: Are we sure the 30% is real and not seasonal?
unresolved_threads: what 'more of this' means concretely
seasonality of the 30% claim

transcript.speaker: A
transcript.text: Here's what I'm seeing. Since the redesign shipped, the support tickets dropped 30%. I think we should do more of this.

transcript.speaker: B
transcript.text: Are we sure the 30% is real and not seasonal? Last year we also saw a drop around the same time.

transcript.speaker: A
transcript.text: Fair, I haven't controlled for that. But the qualitative signal is consistent. People are finding their way around faster.

transcript.speaker: C
transcript.text: I'm supportive in principle. The team is stretched though. If we want to do more, we'd need another two engineers at least.

transcript.speaker: B
transcript.text: Two engineers is a big ask in this environment. Is there a way to do this with the existing team by deprioritizing something?

transcript.speaker: A
transcript.text: We could pause the vendor integration work for a quarter. That was lower priority anyway.

transcript.speaker: C
transcript.text: That's a real tradeoff. Let's not decide it in this room without the vendor integration stakeholder present.
